title: Driving Creek Railway Review
meta_desc: Discover the unique Driving Creek Railway in Coromandel, NZ. Read our honest family review, including prices, age suitability, and insider tips to plan your visit.
intro: The Driving Creek Railway is a one-of-a-kind attraction in Coromandel, NZ, that promises an exciting experience for families. This hand-built narrow-gauge railway takes you on a 50-minute journey through a regenerating native forest, past pottery kilns and sculptures, to a stunning summit viewpoint. But what's it like with kids, and is it worth the cost?

sections.heading: The Experience
sections.body:
Our family's visit to the Driving Creek Railway was an unforgettable adventure. The train ride was smooth, and the scenery was breathtaking, with the kids enjoying the tunnels, viaducts, and switchbacks. The commentary provided by the train driver was informative and engaging, sharing the history and significance of the railway.

The railway's unique features, such as the pottery kilns and sculptures, added to the experience, making it more than just a train ride. The summit viewpoint offered stunning views of the surrounding landscape, and the kids loved the sense of accomplishment when we reached the top.
list: Regenerating native forest and scenic views

sections.heading: Value for Money
sections.body:
With adult tickets priced at $42 and child tickets at $15, the Driving Creek Railway may seem a bit pricey for some families. However, considering the unique experience and the effort that has gone into building and maintaining the railway, we felt it was worth the cost.

That being said, families with younger children may find the experience more suitable for kids aged 4 and above, as the commentary and scenery may not hold the attention of very young children.

sections.heading: Insider Tips and Best Time to Visit
sections.body:
To make the most of your visit, we recommend booking your tickets in advance, especially during peak season. The railway can get busy, and you don't want to miss out on a spot.

The best time to visit is during the summer months (December to February) when the weather is warm and sunny, making the outdoor scenery even more enjoyable. If you're looking for a more relaxed experience, consider visiting during the week instead of on weekends.
list: Book tickets in advance, especially during peak season
Visit during summer months for optimal weather
Consider visiting during the week for a more relaxed experience

sections.heading: How to Book and Save Money
sections.body:
Booking your tickets for the Driving Creek Railway is easy and can be done online through their website or by phone. We recommend checking their website for any special promotions or discounts, especially for families or groups.

Additionally, consider purchasing a family pass, which can sometimes offer better value than individual tickets. Be sure to check the railway's website for up-to-date pricing and booking information.

sections.heading: Pottery Gallery, Café & the Zipline
sections.body:
Driving Creek began life as the working pottery of artist Barry Brickell, and that heritage is still a big part of the visit. Before or after your train ride, browse the on-site pottery gallery and shop, where you can see the kilns and pick up a hand-made piece — a lovely, genuinely local souvenir. There's also a café for coffee, snacks and lunch, so you can easily turn a visit into a relaxed half-day rather than a quick stop.

For families with older, braver kids, Driving Creek also runs a guided zipline (EcoZip-style) experience through the native canopy as a separate add-on — a good option if your children are after a bit more adventure than the train alone provides. Check ages and height requirements when you book.
list: On-site pottery gallery and shop — see the kilns and buy a hand-made piece
Café for coffee, snacks and lunch
Optional guided zipline through the native canopy (separate booking)
Allow a relaxed half-day to enjoy it all

sections.heading: Combine It With the Rest of Coromandel
sections.body: Driving Creek sits just north of Coromandel Town, making it easy to pair with other family favourites on the same trip. Head over to the east coast for Hot Water Beach (dig your own hot pool around low tide) and the scenic Cathedral Cove walk, or stop at The Waterworks — a quirky water-powered playground — on the 309 Road. Because Driving Creek is weather-friendly, it's the perfect activity to slot in on a drizzly Coromandel day while saving the beaches for the sunshine.
list: Hot Water Beach — free, dig a hot pool around low tide
Cathedral Cove — scenic coastal walk (check seasonal access)
The Waterworks — water-powered playground on the 309 Road
Great rainy-day pick — save the beaches for fine weather

sections.heading: Downsides for Families
sections.body:
While the Driving Creek Railway is an excellent attraction for families, there are a few things to consider. The train ride can be a bit long for very young children, and the commentary may not hold their attention. Additionally, the railway's facilities, such as the toilets and picnic areas, are basic but clean and well-maintained.

Overall, we felt that the benefits of visiting the Driving Creek Railway far outweighed the downsides, and we would highly recommend it to families with kids aged 4 and above.
